blocks.content: Mara was on the porch when he got back, wrapped in a blanket with a book and the particular expression of someone who was enjoying the night air but would not admit that the book had been set face-down for some time.

blocks.speaker: Mara
blocks.content: You have a specific face. The one where something registered and you're deciding if it's worth saying.
He sat in the chair beside her. Looked at the section of beach he could see from the porch — dark now, the bonfire around the point of the dune and out of sightline.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: The neighbors. Walked past their bonfire.

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: One of them was watching one of the others in a way that I couldn't account for. The way a person looks at something they've been building toward. It was—

blocks.speaker: Julian
blocks.content: It was probably nothing. People look at each other in strange ways at beach bonfires. Context I don't have. None of my business.

blocks.speaker: Mara
blocks.content: Correct. On vacation. Not your case. Not your problem.
Mara picked her book back up.
Julian looked at the dark section of beach.

There was a specific quality to the look he'd seen — the woman across the fire, the flawless transition when someone spoke to her. He'd seen that quality once before, in the eyes of a man in an interview room who was explaining very calmly where he'd been on a Thursday evening, and Julian had known before the man finished the sentence that every word of it was something he'd constructed well in advance.
